elements.sent-2411-001: How can robots learn and adapt to new tasks and situations with little data? Systematic exploration and simulation are crucial tools for efficient robot learning.
elements.sent-2411-002: We present a novel black-box policy search algorithm focused on data-efficient policy improvements.
elements.sent-2411-003: The algorithm learns directly on the robot and treats simulation as an additional information source to speed up the learning process.
elements.sent-2411-004: At the core of the algorithm, a probabilistic model learns the dependence of the policy parameters and the robot learning objective not only by performing experiments on the robot, but also by leveraging data from a simulator.
elements.sent-2411-005: This substantially reduces interaction time with the robot.
elements.sent-2411-006: Using this model, we can guarantee improvements with high probability for each policy update, thereby facilitating fast, goal-oriented learning.
elements.sent-2411-007: We evaluate our algorithm on simulated fine-tuning tasks and demonstrate the data-efficiency of the proposed dual-information source optimization algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-008: In a real robot learning experiment, we show fast and successful task learning on a robot manipulator with the aid of an imperfect simulator.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=3.5in]{figs/sim_real} \caption{Experiment setup: A robot manipulator is balancing a planar pendulum and learning to follow the reference trajectory with the pendulum.} \label{fig:sim-and-real}
elements.sent-2411-009: However, analytical methods for the design of fine-tuned robot controllers require significant expertise to design and test.
elements.sent-2411-010: Data-driven methods, where robots learn policies and behaviors directly by interacting with their environment, offer an attractive alternative.
elements.sent-2411-011: Learning can enable robots to adapt to complex and dynamic environments, making them more versatile and useful in a wide range of applications.
elements.sent-2411-012: Indeed, over the last decade, machine learning methods, particularly deep reinforcement learning (RL), have enabled robots to solve tasks in difficult and unstructured environments \cite{levine2018learning,openai2019solving,lee2020learning .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Most RL approaches are formulated around state-action pairs and assume a Markovian environment, requiring direct state transitions and actions for their policy updates.
elements.sent-2411-014: Further, the use of uncorrelated random noise in the action space for exploration can lead to jerky motions \cite{ibarz2021train , which are undesirable in real-world settings, since actuators may take a lot of wear and tear during the training.}
elements.sent-2411-015: An alternative to address the above limitations is black-box policy search~\cite{chatzilygeroudis2019survey .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Black-box policy search directly learns a mapping between the policy parameters and the expected return.
elements.sent-2411-017: This alleviates the need to explore directly in the action space and allows tuning episodic policies, such as movement primitives (MPs) \cite{ijspeert2002movement, paraschos2013probabilistic , which lead to smooth trajectories.}
elements.sent-2411-018: Further, it does not require Markov assumptions on states, actions, and rewards.
elements.sent-2411-019: We can, \eg define a reward that depends on time and only consider the final state.
elements.sent-2411-020: This is suitable for robot applications, \eg pick-and-place.
elements.sent-2411-021: The experimental setup for step-based RL on hardware still requires a specialized setup \cite{luo2024serl .}
elements.sent-2411-022: In black-box policy search, the robot experiment can be performed independently of the algorithm since we only need the final trajectory to evaluate the performance.
elements.sent-2411-023: Black-box approaches, however, do not collect and use all state variables, and do not use all available information for tuning \cite{chatzilygeroudis2017black .}
elements.sent-2411-024: Here, Bayesian optimization (BO), a black-box optimization method, has successfully been applied to this problem; however, it is typically only suitable for low dimensional problems \cite{chatzilygeroudis2019survey .}
elements.sent-2411-025: How to apply BO to high-dimensional search spaces is a popular research topic (\eg\cite{eriksson2019scalable, muller2021local, frohlich2021cautious, eriksson2021high, nguyen2022local, ziomek2023random ).}
elements.sent-2411-026: Departing from the standard BO formulation and its limitations, but building on some of its key ideas, we propose a policy search and tuning method that is both data-efficient and suitable for higher dimensional black-box learning problems as it systematically leverages data from a simulator to decrease hardware interaction time.
elements.sent-2411-027: For many robotic systems, simulators are available that yield data at a comparatively much lower cost than real robotic systems in terms of running time, mechanical wear, consumed energy, etc. But, since models are imperfect, the learned behavior will be sub-optimal when applied to the real system.
elements.sent-2411-028: This motivates learning, at least in part, on the robot directly.
elements.sent-2411-029: To determine the trade-off between simulation and robot data, we propose to model the uncertainty of the simulated interaction and use Bayesian decision-making to decide on the information source (see \fig\ref{fig:sim-and-real ).}
elements.sent-2411-030: Specifically, the learner shall guarantee local policy improvement and iteratively decide which information source should be used to provide the desired guarantees.
elements.sent-2411-031: If the simulation data is informative, we avoid data collection in the physical world, decreasing the overall cost of data acquisition.
elements.sent-2411-032: However, once the information from the simulation is insufficient to further improve the policy on the current task, data from the robot will be needed.
elements.sent-2411-033: Following a policy change, the simulator might once more prove to be a valuable information source.
elements.sent-2411-034: This interactive approach differs from existing methods that leverage simulators and tackle the problem of the so-called reality gap using, \eg domain randomization~\cite{james2017transferring, peng2018sim, andrychowicz2020learning or domain adaption~\cite{gupta2017learning, rusu2017sim, tanwani2021dirl , as in our method the learner actively decides which data source it needs to improve the policy for the robot (not the simulation) during the learning process.}}
elements.sent-2411-035: We propose a general and data-efficient zero-order local search method suitable for black-box learning problems often encountered in robot applications.
elements.sent-2411-036: The method, based on \citet{muller2021local , employs zero-order and multi-fidelity information, where a probabilistic model directly learns the dependence between policy parameters and the robot learning objective.}
elements.sent-2411-038: An efficient decision rule for policy updates with multi-fidelity local BO that ensures a high probability of improvement at every iteration.
elements.sent-2411-039: Evaluations of the suggested local search method on both synthetic benchmarks and robotic tasks.
elements.sent-2411-040: Our method is especially well-suited to fine-tuning tasks or deployment in dynamic environments where fast adaptation is required and robots must learn and adapt with minimal interaction time.
elements.sent-2411-041: The dual-information source optimization, which leverages both real-world data and simulations, enables fast improvements on hardware through guided exploration and simulations.. \newcommand{\figblock {.}}
elements.sent-2411-042: The closed-loop system is parameterized through a vector $\theta \in \Theta$ that lies in a bounded and closed $\Theta \subset \mathbb{R ^d$.}
elements.sent-2411-043: The parameters $\theta$ represent any configurable property of the robotic system.
elements.sent-2411-044: Specifically, in the experimental sections of this paper we use the parameters to define a MP.
elements.sent-2411-045: We denote a trajectory that was obtained with a specific configuration $\theta$ as $\tau_\theta$.
elements.sent-2411-046: We define the learning task in terms of a performance function $\obj : (\mathcal{S \times \mathcal{A )^K \mapsto \mathbb{R}$ that maps a trajectory of the closed-loop to a scalar.}}
elements.sent-2411-047: The episodic policy search problem is cast as a maximization problem over $\obj$, \begin{equation}\label{eq:min_cost} \theta^* = \!\argmax\limits_{\theta \in \Theta} \obj(\tau_\theta).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-048: For notational convenience we use $\obj(\tau_\theta)$ and $\obj(\theta)$ interchangeably.
elements.sent-2411-049: We solve the policy search problem by sampling trajectories $\tau$ with different policy parameterizations (\cf\fig\ref{fig:block_diagram ), where each sample obtained through an experiment.}
elements.sent-2411-050: After the \(i\)-th experiment, we receive a noisy performance sample \begin{equation}y_i = \obj(\tau_{\theta_i}) + \epsilon_i,\end{equation} where we assume $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N (0,\sigma^2_n)$.}
elements.sent-2411-051: The reason for noisy evaluations are, for example, stochastic dynamics or random initial conditions.
elements.sent-2411-052: In contrast to classical optimization and RL, we consider the policy search problem as a black-box optimization problem and only work with tuples $(\theta, y)$.
elements.sent-2411-053: Importantly, we do not require an analytical expression of $\obj$ or any first-order information or samples, \ie $\nabla \obj$ is unknown, and we do not require assumptions on the level of the underlying dynamical system or policies.
elements.sent-2411-054: In addition to trajectories from the robotic system, the algorithm can acquire additional data from an imperfect simulator \begin{equation}\label{eq:sim} \obj_\mathrm{sim}(\theta) = \obj(\theta) + \obj_\mathrm{gap}(\theta),\end{equation} with an unknown reality gap $\obj_\mathrm{gap}$.
elements.sent-2411-055: The performance on the simulator will differ from that on the robot.
elements.sent-2411-056: This difference constitutes a bias and data from a policy evaluated with \eqref{eq:sim leads to a biased estimate $\obj_\mathrm{sim (\theta)$ of $\obj(\theta)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-057: To build a probabilistic surrogate model for Bayesian decision making, we assume some prior knowledge of the functions $\obj$, $\obj_\mathrm{sim}$, and their correlations.
elements.sent-2411-058: Specifically, we assume $\obj$ and the reality gap $\obj_\mathrm{gap = \obj - \obj_\mathrm{sim}$ are samples from known Gaussian process (GP) priors.}
elements.sent-2411-059: Since GPs are closed under addition, $\obj_\mathrm{sim}$ is also a GP.
elements.sent-2411-060: This assumption that the objective functions are samples from a GP is standard in BO (\cf \cite{srinivas2010gaussian ).}
elements.sent-2411-061: Further, the derivatives of $\obj$ are Lipschitz continuous with constant \(L > 0\), i.e., we have that \( \|\nabla \obj(a) - \nabla \obj(b)\| \leq L\|a - b\| \) for any \(a, b\).. \end{assumption.}
elements.sent-2411-062: Our work provides a method for data-efficient robot learning that builds on Bayesian decision-making, as well as learning through simulation.
elements.sent-2411-063: This section provides an overview of related work in these areas.
elements.sent-2411-064: There are currently two main strategies for improving data-efficiency in robot learning (\cf \cite{chatzilygeroudis2019survey ).}
elements.sent-2411-065: One is to build prior knowledge, \eg of the policy representation, into a learning system.
elements.sent-2411-066: The other is to learn the robot dynamics and optimize the policy w.r.t.\ the learned model.
elements.sent-2411-067: This strategy is known as model-based policy search.
elements.sent-2411-068: Prior knowledge for policy representation is often motivated by control theory, where human experts decide which control design is suitable for the given task~\cite{berkenkamp2016safe,marco2016automatic,edwards2021automatic,guzman2022bayesian .}
elements.sent-2411-069: The learning task then consists of tuning the parameters of the chosen policy representation and is thus frequently called ``controller tuning''.
elements.sent-2411-070: Choosing such a parameterization requires specific knowledge of the system dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-071: Further, prior knowledge can also be encoded into trajectory-based policies.
elements.sent-2411-072: In this setting, the policy is designed as a sequence of way-points~\cite{jain2020computing or uses function approximators to mimic system dynamics~\cite{solak2019learning .}}
elements.sent-2411-073: In the latter case, MPs are the most widely used policy structure.
elements.sent-2411-074: Located at a higher level, \ie a task level, trajectory-based policies can be generated by a human expert or by a pre-trained RL model \cite{otto2023deep .}
elements.sent-2411-075: It is a common practice to have a low-level controller to facilitate the trajectory tracking.
elements.sent-2411-076: In these cases, an optimizer tries to fine-tune the trajectories for the given robotic tasks.
elements.sent-2411-077: In the robot example of this paper, we leverage these policy parameterizations and learn to improve a task trajectory encoded by MPs to achieve better task performance.
elements.sent-2411-078: To make RL more data-efficient model-based policy search algorithms learn a model of the dynamics~\cite{deisenroth2011pilco,chatzilygeroudis2017black,chua2018deep, buckman2018sample, janner2019when, yang2020data, whitman2023learning and optimize the policy with respect to this model.}
elements.sent-2411-079: However, these algorithms are not designed for the episodic, black-box setting we consider herein and need additional assumptions on the dynamics and reward signal.
elements.sent-2411-080: Bayesian optimization, \ie using Bayesian decision theory to find the optimizer of a function in a data-efficient way, has been re-discovered multiple times in various communities.
elements.sent-2411-081: We focus here on BO for robot learning and refer the reader to \citet{garnett2023bayesian for an in-depth introduction to BO.}
elements.sent-2411-082: In policy search for robot learning, BO uses Bayesian inference and a sequential design of experiments (DoE) to find policies in a data-efficient way.
elements.sent-2411-083: Bayesian optimization was first applied to robot learning to solve problems in the form of \eqref{eq:min_cost by \citet{lizotte2007automatic , where a quadruped robot learned gaits by optimizing the parameters of a walk engine.}}
elements.sent-2411-084: Since then, BO has been successfully applied to robot learning \cite{marco2016automatic, driess2017constrained, marco2017virtual, frohlich2021cautious .}
elements.sent-2411-085: These prior works show that BO is a viable tool for data-efficient robot learning, motivating our adoption of a similar approach.
elements.sent-2411-086: However, the described methods are only applicable to policies with a relatively small number of policy parameters (\cf\cite{chatzilygeroudis2019survey ) as they search globally for the optimal policy and global searches are highly susceptible to the curse of dimensionality.}
elements.sent-2411-087: With limited interaction time global search is likely to be outperformed by a local search \cite{muller2021local,frohlich2021cautious .}
elements.sent-2411-088: The local search method presented in this paper does not solve the global optimization problem.
elements.sent-2411-089: Instead, it searches for a local improvement of a given parameter.
elements.sent-2411-090: In local search variants of BO the search is restricted using trust-regions \cite{eriksson2019scalable , local search distributions \cite{akrour2017local , or uncertainty about the objective \cite{frohlich2021cautious .}}}
elements.sent-2411-091: Instead of constraining the search space to force local exploration, we reformulate the objective for the sequential design of experiments.
elements.sent-2411-092: The new formulation is based on a DoE algorithm, first proposed in \cite{muller2021local , that finds a search direction in the parameter space to improve the current guess of the best policy.}
elements.sent-2411-093: In contrast to BO, the data is used to optimally reduce the uncertainty of the objective's gradient at the current guess of the best policy in \cite{muller2021local or to maximize the probability of improvement in \cite{nguyen2022local .}}
elements.sent-2411-094: We extend these results to obtain probabilistic guarantees for policy improvement.
elements.sent-2411-095: We then adaptively choose how much data is needed for these guarantees at each policy update, thereby avoiding unnecessary interaction and further increasing the data-efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-096: Sim-to-real robot learning is often understood as first learning a policy in simulation, and then transferring it to the robot.
elements.sent-2411-097: Unfortunately, the reality gap---the mismatch between the simulation and the real system---can impede such a transfer.
elements.sent-2411-098: Numerous algorithms have been proposed to tackle the sim-to-real transfer problem.
elements.sent-2411-099: One popular approach to account for the bias in the simulator is domain randomization \cite{tobin2017domain, james2017transferring, peng2018sim, andrychowicz2020learning (see \citet{muratore2022robot for a survey).}}
elements.sent-2411-100: Domain randomization varies the parameters of the simulator and learns a policy that is robust to these variations.
elements.sent-2411-101: The underlying assumption is that the variations will account for the bias of the simulator.
elements.sent-2411-102: By contrast, we account for the bias of the simulator by assuming imperfect correlations in the expected return of a policy.
elements.sent-2411-103: Then, instead of increasing the variance of the simulator and learning a robust policy, we model both returns probabilistically and reason about the probability of improvement on the robot.
elements.sent-2411-104: We can then sequentially decide where to query data.
elements.sent-2411-105: Our proposed method is closely related to the work by \citet{marco2017virtual , where a dual-information source GP model is proposed; however, the algorithm in \cite{marco2017virtual is a global search method, and thus does not scale well with the dimensionality of the policy.}}
elements.sent-2411-106: Instead, we propose a local search where we improve the current guess of the best policy on the robot.

elements.sent-2411-110: This posterior distribution can be computed analytically, which, following \citet{muller2021local we will use to form a belief over the function's derivative from noisy zeroth-order observations.}

elements.subsection-2411-002: Sequential Policy Search with Gradient Information
elements.sent-2411-113: Sequential black box policy search (cf.~\fig\ref{fig:block_diagram ) requires both a query function and a decision function.}
elements.sent-2411-114: We build our algorithm on Gradient Information with BO (GIBO)~\cite{muller2021local .}
elements.sent-2411-115: This method maintains a current guess of the best policy $\hat \theta_*$ and queries the objective to find a search direction that improves this guess.
elements.sent-2411-116: A GIBO query is defined as the observation that optimally reduces a measure of the total uncertainty of the gradient for the current guess of the best policy $\Sigma_*^\prime$ \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{eq:gi_query} Query(\mathcal{D},\hat \theta_*) \coloneqq \argmax_{\theta \in \Theta} \alpha_\text{GI}(\theta\mid \mathcal{D}, \hat \theta_*), \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\alpha_\text{GI}$ is the Gradient Information (GI) acquisition function \begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\label{eq:gi_acquistion} \alpha_\text{GI}(\theta\mid\mathcal{D}, \hat \theta_*) = \E{[ \Tr(\Sigma_*^\prime\mid\mathcal{D}) - \Tr(\Sigma_*^\prime\mid\mathcal{D} \cup (\theta, y)) ]} = \Tr \left(\nabla K\left(\hat \theta_*,\queriesvirtual\right) \left(K\left(\queriesvirtual,\queriesvirtual\right) + \sigma_n^2 I \right)^{-1} \right. \qquad\qquad\qquad\left. \nabla K(\hat\theta_*, \queriesvirtual)\right)\tran, \end{aligned}\end{equation} with $\Sigma_*^\prime$ the gradient variance before and after observing a new point $(\theta, y)$, $\queriesvirtual = \queries \cup {\theta}$.
elements.sent-2411-117: Critically, the posterior variance does not depend on the observation $y$.
elements.sent-2411-118: Hence, the optimal query can be determined without knowing its outcome.
elements.sent-2411-119: Here, the trace is taken as a measure of total variance, but other measures are possible.
elements.sent-2411-121: The original GIBO algorithm now makes $M$ such queries, where $M$ is set to be the dimensions of the policy domain, before updating $\hat\theta_*$ using the mean estimate of the gradient \begin{equation}\label{eq:update_rule} \hat\theta_{*} \leftarrow \hat\theta_{*} - \eta \mu_{*}^\prime,\end{equation} with step size $\eta$.
elements.sent-2411-122: Increasing $M$ could slow down the optimization since using a larger number of samples does not necessarily provide any additional valuable gradient information when an accurate estimation has been achieved.
elements.sent-2411-123: On the other hand, if $M$ is reduced too much, while it might help avoid unnecessary queries and improve efficiency, there is a risk of erroneous gradient estimates.
elements.sent-2411-124: A core contribution of our work is to replace the hyperparameter $M$ with a condition on the probability of improvement, meaning we query as long as necessary to guarantee an improved policy with high probability.
elements.section-2411-005: High-Confidence Policy Improvements with Gradient Information Bayesian Optimization
elements.sent-2411-125: In this section, we introduce the Gradient Information with BO (GIBO) framework \cite{muller2021local .}
elements.sent-2411-126: We then propose our own algorithm High-Confidence Improvement GIBO (HCI-GIBO), which introduces an efficient decision rule for when to update the current guess of the best policy $\hat \theta_*$.
elements.sent-2411-127: Furthermore, we extend the method such that it can leverage data from an additional biased information source such as a simulator (S-HCI-GIBO).
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{GIBO (adapted from \cite{muller2021local})}\label{algo:gibo} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State Hyperparameters: hyperpriors for GP hyperparameters, improvement confidence $\alpha$, stepsize $\eta$, \State $\hat{\theta}_* \leftarrow \theta_0$, $\mathcal{D} \leftarrow \mathcal{D}_0$ \Repeat \Comment{Policy updates} \Repeat \Comment{DoE for descent direction} \State $\theta_i \leftarrow Query(\mathcal{D}, \hat\theta_*)$ \State $y_i \leftarrow f(\theta_i) + \epsilon_i$ \State $\mathcal{D} \leftarrow \mathcal{D} \cup (\theta_i, y_i)$ \Until{$Commitment(\mathcal{D}, \hat\theta_*)\geq \alpha$} \State $\nabla f \leftarrow DescentDirection(\mathcal{D}, \hat\theta_*)$ \State $\hat\theta_* \leftarrow \hat\theta_* - \eta \, \nabla f$ \Comment{Optional: normalized gradient descent} \Until{$Termination(\mathcal{D}, \hat\theta_*)$} \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-128: The general GIBO framework is summarized in Alg.~\ref{algo:gibo .}
elements.sent-2411-129: Just like BO, GIBO requires hyperpriors for GP hyperparameters.
elements.sent-2411-130: For the SE kernel, GP hyperparameters are lengthscales that describe how relevant an input is.
elements.sent-2411-131: In practice, hyperpriors, \ie prior distributions of hyperparameters, are determined through domain knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-132: A common approach to obtain such experience is to collect a small dataset before the optimization.
elements.sent-2411-133: During the optimization, hyperparameters (lengthscales) can be adjusted by maximum a posteriori estimation (see chapter 5 of \cite{rasmussen2006gaussian for details).}
elements.sent-2411-134: The algorithm relies on three decisions, namely, a Query function that decides where to query the objective function next; a Commitment condition that decides when to terminate the query process; and a DescentDirection function that guides the gradient descent to improve the policy.
elements.sent-2411-135: These three elements, which we will make precise for HCI-GIBO, operate in two nested loops.
elements.sent-2411-136: In the inner loop, we query the objective function.
elements.sent-2411-137: Once the commitment condition is triggered, we leave the inner loop and take a gradient step with the learned descent direction.
elements.sent-2411-138: For our work, the derivative GP model (Sec.~\ref{sec:gp_model ) serves as a proxy to approximate the unknown objective and to derive an analytical distribution for the objective’s derivative.}
elements.sent-2411-139: Then, we use the gradient information introduced in Sec.~\ref{sec:gradient as the Query function, to actively locate the most informative query points for the gradient of the current guess of the best policy.}
elements.sent-2411-140: Compared with GIBO, we reduce data redundancies by deriving a criterion to quantify the improvement confidence and to terminate the query process.
elements.sent-2411-141: This improvement confidence serves as Commitment in HCI-GIBO.
elements.sent-2411-142: The Bayesian design of experiments to infer the gradients in GIBO algorithms contributes to data efficiency by actively querying data to improve the estimates.
elements.sent-2411-143: Intuitively, more data leads to a better gradient estimate.
elements.sent-2411-144: But how much data or knowledge does the optimizer require to improve the policy? In previous work \cite{muller2021local,nguyen2022local , the number of queries is a hyperparameter\footnote{\citet{nguyen2022local use a fixed number of queries, but make multiple steps.. .}}}
elements.sent-2411-145: By contrast, HCI-GIBO queries the function until it can guarantee that the update step will improve the function with high probability.
elements.sent-2411-146: This avoids early updates that might lead to a decrease in performance and the collection of unnecessary data.
elements.sent-2411-147: To guarantee improvement, we first define the set of all vectors that improve the objective.
elements.sent-2411-148: Let \(\nabla f\) be the gradient of \(f\) and \(\direction\) the descent direction.
elements.sent-2411-149: Then, for all \(\direction\) and a given fixed step size $\eta$, the function improves, \ie \begin{equation}\label{eqn:deterministic_improvement_claim} f(\theta - \eta\direction) < f(\theta)\end{equation} if \begin{equation}\label{eq:criteria} \left\langle \frac{\direction}{\|\direction\|}, \nabla f \right\rangle > \frac{L }{2} \eta \|\direction\|.\end{equation}.

elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{S-HCI-GIBO}\label{algo:s-hci-gibo} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State Hyperparameters: hyperpriors for GP hyperparameters, improvement confidence $\alpha$, SimToReal threshold $\beta$, stepsize $\eta$, \State $\hat\theta_* \leftarrow \theta_0$, $\mathcal{D} \leftarrow \mathcal{D}_0$ \Repeat \Comment{Policy updates} \State $\IS \leftarrow \IS_{\simu}$ \Comment{Start with $\IS_{\simu}$} \Repeat \Comment{DoE for descent direction} \State $\theta_i \leftarrow Query(\mathcal{D}, \hat{\theta}_*, \IS)$ \If{$\IS$ is $\IS_{\simu}$} \State $y_i \leftarrow f_{\simu}(\theta_i)+\epsilon_i$ \Else \State $y_i \leftarrow f(\theta_i)+\epsilon_i$ \EndIf \State $\mathcal{D} \leftarrow \mathcal{D} \cup (\theta_i, y_i, \IS)$ \If{$SimToReal(\mathcal{D}, \hat\theta_*, \IS) \leq \beta$ } \State $\IS \leftarrow \IS_{\real}$ \Comment{Switch from simulator to robot} \EndIf \Until{$Commitment(\mathcal{D}, \hat\theta_*, \IS_{\real}) \geq \alpha$} \State $\mu^\prime_* \leftarrow DescentDirection(\mathcal{D}, \hat\theta_*, \IS_{\real})$ \State $\hat\theta_* \leftarrow \hat\theta_* - \eta \, \mu^\prime_*$ \Comment{Optional: normalized gradient descent} \Until{$Termination(\mathcal{D}, \hat\theta_*)$} \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-168: Simulators are ubiquitous, fast, and play a vital role in exposing robots to learning tasks in the safety of the virtual world.
elements.sent-2411-169: In the following, we first give a high-level overview of S-HCI-GIBO, and then introduce the algorithm's details.
elements.sent-2411-170: S-HCI-GIBO, as summarized in Alg.~\ref{algo:s-hci-gibo , follows the general GIBO framework with its two nested loops but includes two information sources that can be queried in the inner loop.}
elements.sent-2411-171: In this algorithm, we use a binary indicator \(\IS\) and denote the simulator and the real robot by \(\IS_{\simu}\coloneqq 0\) and \(\IS_{\real \coloneqq 1\), respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-172: S-HCI-GIBO will Query the simulator first until the SimToReal function reaches a preset threshold.
elements.sent-2411-173: The threshold indicates data from the simulator is no longer significant for the Commitment condition.
elements.sent-2411-174: Then, the optimizer will Query the robot.
elements.sent-2411-175: When the threshold in Commitment is reached, the optimizer leaves the inner loop.
elements.sent-2411-176: Note that it is possible to achieve the Commitment condition only with data from a simulator.
elements.sent-2411-177: We propose a dual information source derivative GP model to model \(f\) and \(f_{\simu}\) and extend the GI for a dual information source as the Query function.
elements.sent-2411-178: The Commitment and DescentDirection functions are identical to HCI-GIBO.
elements.sent-2411-179: The dual information source derivative GP model and the SimToReal switching rule are introduced in the following.
elements.subsubsection-2411-002: Dual information source derivative GP model
elements.sent-2411-180: Following the model \eqref{eq:model_GIBO , and motivated by Poloczek et al. \cite{poloczek2017multi , we construct a model with a distinctive kernel that enables the transition of queries between different information sources.}}
elements.sent-2411-181: We extend the parameter vector \( \theta\) by a binary indicator \(\IS\).
elements.sent-2411-183: The covariance matrix \(K\left(\queriesext, x_*\right)\) has the form \cite{marco2017virtual \begin{equation}K\left(\queriesext, x_*\right) = K_f(\theta, \hat\theta_*) + k_\delta({\IS}, {\IS}_*) K_{m}( \theta, \hat\theta_*). \label{eq:new_kernel}\end{equation} In \eqref{eq:new_kernel , \(K_f\) and \(K_m\) are given by two kernel functions $k: \Theta \times \Theta \mapsto \mathbb{R}$, in which the kernel hyperparameters can be set respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-184: Given the derivative GP model \eqref{eq:model_GIBO , conditioning the GP's prior distribution on \(\{\queriesext, \bar{y}\}\) leads to the posterior distribution, which can be readily obtained by substituting \(K(\queries, \hat \theta_*)\) into \eqref{eq:posterior_derivative_gp with the new kernel \(K(\queriesext, x_*)\) in \eqref{eq:new_kernel .}}}
elements.sent-2411-185: We implement the GI acquisition function for both information sources.
elements.sent-2411-186: For any parameter $\theta$, there should now exist points on both \(\IS_{\simu}\) and \(\IS_{\real}\) that are informative for its respective gradients.
elements.sent-2411-187: Thus, at each timepoint the algorithm can decide to query either the simulator or the real robot.
elements.sent-2411-188: Experiments on the real robot are typically more informative than simulations.
elements.sent-2411-189: Thus, when maximizing gradient information without consideration of the cost, the algorithm will mostly neglect the simulator.
elements.sent-2411-190: However, the information from the simulator is cheap as it requires no interaction time with the robot.
elements.sent-2411-191: We therefore establish a hierarchy and first exhaust gradient information from the simulator before turning to the real robot.
elements.sent-2411-192: Given a derivative GP model, the data-set \(\mathcal{D}\) of the observation, and the current guess of the best policy \( \hat \theta_*\), we revisit and rewrite the utility in \eqref{eq:gi_acquistion as the expected difference between the derivative's variance before and after observing a new point \((x, y)\), which contains the policy \(\theta\), the information source \(\IS\), and the performance observation \(y\) \begin{equation}\label{eq:acq} \begin{aligned} \alpha_{\rm{S}-\rm{GI}}\left(x \mid \mathcal D, (\hat\theta_*, {\IS}_{\real})\right) = \E \bigl[\Tr \bigl(\Sigma_*^\prime \mid \mathcal{D}\bigr) - \Tr\bigl(\Sigma_*^\prime \mid \mathcal{D}\cup\left(x, y\right) \bigr)\bigr], \end{aligned}\end{equation} where \(\Sigma_*^\prime\) is the variance of the derivative GP model at the pair \((\hat\theta_*, \IS_{\real )\).}}
elements.sent-2411-193: Note that we aim to reduce the gradient uncertainty of the policy on the real robot, and therefore $\alpha_{\rm{S -\rm{GI }$ depends on the GP variance for ${\rm IS _{\rm real}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-194: The posterior at the policy \(\hat\theta_*\) can be conditioned on data from both \(f\) and \(f_{\simu}\).
elements.sent-2411-195: Compared to acquisition function GI \eqref{eq:gi_acquistion , \(\alpha_{\rm{S -\rm{GI }\) calculates the gradient information for $\IS_{\real}$ from a policy executed on either a real robot or a simulator.}}}
elements.sent-2411-196: Note that for the same policy \(\hat \theta_*\), \(\alpha_{\rm{S -\rm{GI }\) varies in terms of the selected information source.}}
elements.sent-2411-197: We are now ready to introduce a threshold to indicate when the algorithm should query the real robot instead of the simulator.
elements.sent-2411-198: This can be quantified by measuring the utility function for the neighboring query steps on the simulator.
elements.sent-2411-199: Consider queries in one gradient step.
elements.sent-2411-200: We initially execute our queries on the simulator.
elements.sent-2411-201: This approach enables us to fully exploit the gradient information gained from the simulator, and effectively decreases the need for samples from the real robot.
elements.sent-2411-202: Given the query step \(i\), the data set $\mathcal{D}$, and the current guess of the best policy \(\hat \theta_*\).
elements.sent-2411-203: Then the difference between the utility at step \(i\) and \(i+1\) on the simulator is \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \label{eq:simtoreal_switching} SimToReal \coloneqq \alpha_{S-GI}\left(\left(\theta_{i+1}, IS_{sim}\right)\mid \mathcal D, (\hat\theta_*, {\IS}_{\real})\right) - \alpha_{S-GI}\left(\left(\theta_{i}, IS_{sim}\right)\mid \mathcal D, (\hat\theta_*, {\IS}_{\real})\right) \end{aligned}\end{equation} This measures how much the variance will change for the gradient if such query point is included in the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-204: We introduce a threshold \(\beta\) as a hyperparameter for the sim to real switching.
elements.sent-2411-205: When $\beta$ is small, more queries on the simulation will be included, and it could possibly further decrease the number of samples needed from the real robot.
elements.sent-2411-206: However, this will increase overall training time.

elements.sent-2411-215: In \fig\ref{fig:confidence , we show the regions (dots) in which the projections of unknown true gradients on its mean vector (arrows) fulfill \eqref{eq:criteria .}}

elements.subsection-2411-012: Learning from Scratch: Impromptu Trajectory Tracking
elements.sent-2411-323: Trajectory tracking control is a fundamental and essential aspect of robotic systems, yet designing and fine-tuning classical controllers can be rather labor-intensive with hidden dynamics, making it challenging to achieve optimal tracking precision~\cite{li2017deep .}
elements.sent-2411-324: In this experiment, we investigate how our proposed methods learn to improve the tracking performance of such robotic systems.
elements.sent-2411-325: We choose a robotic pole-balancing setting, where the robotic arm has two different level control tasks: (i) to ensure that the pole does not fall over (low level), and (ii) to get the tip of the pole to precisely track a preset trajectory (task level).
elements.sent-2411-326: The predefined trajectory is only encoded in a performance function at the task level, and the low level pendulum balancing control has no prior knowledge of the predefined trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-327: The main motivation for robotic pole-balancing setting is that it emulates universal robot trajectory tracking in an abstract and fairly easy way, since sophisticated tasks are usually built upon low level robot kinematic control.
elements.sent-2411-328: Further, the problem of planning, in particular with unknown nonlinear dynamics, at the task level can be treated as a search problem.
elements.sent-2411-329: The optimizer operates at the task level, and learns to improve the tracking performance.
elements.sent-2411-330: The test bed setup, shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:setup , consists of a Franka Emika Robot, a planar pendulum, and a Vicon optical motion capture system.}
elements.sent-2411-331: The Franka Emika robot is a seven-joint open-chain manipulator that serves as a cart in a classic pendulum balancing problem.
elements.sent-2411-332: The current state of the robot and the torque control command are transmitted via the Franka Control Interface at \SI{1 {\kilo\hertz .}}
elements.sent-2411-333: For our experiment, we published an open-source ROS package\footnote{The code of pendulum balancing is available at \href{https://github.com/Data-Science-in-Mechanical-Engineering/franka_pendulum {https://github.com/Data-Science-in-Mechanical-Engineering/franka\_pendulum .}}}
elements.sent-2411-334: The pendulum is a stick with a needle to yield low-friction contact to the end-effector.
elements.sent-2411-335: The end-effector can move in all directions in the horizontal plane (see \fig\ref{fig:needle ).}
elements.sent-2411-336: The Vicon motion capture system evaluates the position of special spherical markers and considers an object as an array of marker coordinates in the object reference frame.
elements.sent-2411-337: A transformation from the object coordinate to the world coordinate is applied so that we obtain measurements of the pole angle and estimates of the angular velocity by numerical differentiation at \SI{100 {\hertz .}}
elements.sent-2411-338: Similar to \cite{schaal1996learning, marco2016automatic , the dynamics of the balancing problem can be described as: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} ml^2 I \begin{bmatrix} \ddot \phi \ddot \theta \end{bmatrix} - mgl I \begin{bmatrix} \sin \phi \sin \theta \end{bmatrix} + mgl I \begin{bmatrix} \cos \phi u_x \cos \theta u_y \end{bmatrix} + \xi \begin{bmatrix} \dot \phi \dot \theta \end{bmatrix} = 0, \begin{bmatrix} \ddot x \ddot y \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} u_x u_y \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} a_x a_y \end{bmatrix}, \end{aligned} \label{eq:pole}\end{equation} where \(I\) is an identity matrix, \(\bm q = [\phi, \theta]\tran\) is the pole angle vector from the vertical line in two directions, \(\bm u = [u_x, u_y]\tran\) is the end-effector acceleration vector, and \(\bm \eta = [x, y]\tran\) is the distance vector of the base from its origin \((x_0, y_0)=(\SI{0.5 {\m ,\SI{0 {\m )\).}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-024: \begin{equation} ml^2 I  \ddot \phi \ddot \theta  - mgl I  \sin \phi \sin \theta  + mgl I  \cos \phi u_x \cos \theta u_y  + \xi  \dot \phi \dot \theta  = 0,  \ddot x \ddot y  =  u_x u_y  +  a_x a_y ,  \label{eq:pole}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-339: The pole mass \(m\) is \SI{0.1593 {\kg , and the center of mass \(l\) lies at \SI{0.463 {\m .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-340: The applied control law to the pendulum can be written as \begin{equation}\label{eq:pole_controller} \bm u = F\bm x + \bm a.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-341: In \eqref{eq:pole_controller , $F\bm x$ is state-feedback control that stabilizes the pole at the origin, using a well-established discrete-time Linear Quadratic Regulator (LQR), where \(\bm x = [\phi, \dot \phi, x, \dot x, \theta, \dot \theta, y, \dot y]\tran\) is the full state vector of the balancing system.}
elements.sent-2411-342: The control gain \(F\) is computed by solving the LQR problem to minimize \begin{equation}f_{\rm LQR} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \left(x\tran Q x + u\tran R u\right){\rm d}t,\end{equation} where \(Q = 10 I, R = I\).
elements.formula-2411-026: \begin{equation}f_{\rm LQR} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \left(x\tran Q x + u\tran R u\right){\rm d}t,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-344: Further, in \eqref{eq:pole_controller we use DMPs to generate the extra acceleration $\bm a = [a_x, a_y]\tran$ to the stabilized inverted pole.}
elements.sent-2411-345: A rhythmic DMP as proposed in \cite{ijspeert2013dynamical has been adopted to generate the periodic acceleration.}
elements.sent-2411-346: The external acceleration \(a_x\) is \begin{equation}a_x = \frac{\sum^N_{i=1}\Phi_i(t) w_i}{\sum^N_{i=1}\Phi_i(t)} r,\end{equation} where \(\Phi_i(t) = \exp(h_i(\cos(\omega t - c_i)-1))\) are von Mises basis functions that are centered at constants \(c_i\), \(r\) is the amplitude, and \(w_i\) are adjustable weights.
elements.sent-2411-347: In the experiment, we set \(r = 1, h = 1\), and the \(c_i\) are evenly placed in \([0, T_s)\).
elements.sent-2411-348: We adopt the same DMP with twelve basis functions for \(a_x\) and \(a_y\) so that the planar policy is parameterized by 24 weights \(w_i\).
elements.sent-2411-349: The initial value of each weight is 0, \ie $a = 0$, and the robot will hold the pole at its origin during the first rollout.
elements.sent-2411-350: The control input \(\bm u\), also the reference acceleration of the gripper, in (\ref{eq:pole_controller ) is then generated by the end-effector of the Franka Emika robot, realized by Cartesian space controller.}
elements.sent-2411-351: By learning parameters of the DMPs, the optimizer tries to make the tip of the pole follow a reference trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-352: We define the reference trajectory as a Lissajous curve: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} x_d = 0.2 \sin(\omega t)\cos(\omega t) + 0.5, y_d = 0.12 \sin(\omega t), \end{aligned}\end{equation} where \(t \in [0, T_s),\omega = 2\pi / T_s\).
elements.sent-2411-353: For each rollout, the robot tracks the trajectory for a fixed time \(T_s = \) \SI{20 {\second .}}
elements.sent-2411-354: In this way, the end-effector will move back to the origin at the end of each trial, saving the time for initializing the test bed before each rollout.
elements.sent-2411-355: The performance of a rollout on the robot is defined by the cost function \begin{equation}f = \int_0^{T_s} \left(1.2 \|x-x_{\mathrm{d}}\|_2 + \|y - y_{\mathrm{d}}\|_2\right){\rm d}t. \label{eq:reward}\end{equation} We discretized the robot-pendulum tracking system using zero-order hold with a frequency of \SI{100 {\hertz .}}
elements.sent-2411-356: As such each rollout contains 2000 time steps.
elements.sent-2411-357: Norms are weighted to encourage simultaneous growth in both directions.
elements.sent-2411-358: We adopt three different optimizer settings in the experiment: (i) HCI-GIBO on the real robot, (ii) S-HCI-GIBO on the real robot with the help of a simulator, and (iii) HCI-GIBO on the simulator (sim only).
elements.sent-2411-359: For the setting (iii), the final policy learned on the simulator is evaluated on the real robot.
elements.sent-2411-360: We perform five consecutive trials for each setting to report average performance and the deviation.
elements.sent-2411-361: These settings will help us confirm whether the simulator can improve data efficiency by comparing (i) and (ii), and whether the SimToReal is vital to achieve the learning goal by comparing (ii) and (iii).
elements.sent-2411-362: For the HCI-GIBO with no simulator, we use the SE kernel \begin{equation}K(\theta_1, \theta_2)= \nu^2 \exp \left(-\|\theta_1-\theta_2\|^2/(2\lambda) \right),\end{equation} with hand-tuned variance $\nu=2$ and lengthscale $\lambda=0.3$ for all 24 dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-363: For the S-HCI-GIBO, we use the kernel introduced in \eqref{eq:new_kernel .}
elements.sent-2411-364: Note that in \eqref{eq:new_kernel , $K_f$ and $K_m$ are both SE kernels, and in our experiment, the variance and lengthscale for $K_f$ are the same as the HCI-GIBO setting.}
elements.sent-2411-366: The improvement confidence $\alpha$ and the stepsize $\eta$ for all optimizers is 0.95 and 0.2, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-367: The SimToReal switching threshold $\beta$ for S-HCI-GIBO is $1$.

We also run the HCI-GIBO on the simulator, which is denoted by the light-blue dot (``sim only''), and then deploy the learned policy on the real robot. Transferring the policy learned on the simulator cannot achieve fine performance. The shaded regions reported on the x-axis show the standard deviation. } \label{fig:experiment_reward}

elements.sent-2411-368: Since the robot learns the policy from scratch and the algorithm improves the performance after each policy update, we define the performance of the initial policy as the ``worst'' performance while the ``best'' performance based on \eqref{eq:reward is 0.}
elements.sent-2411-369: We use the possible maximum reward increase in \fig\ref{fig:experiment_reward so that it is consistent with the results from the synthetic experiments.}
elements.sent-2411-370: The training curves indicate that S-HCI-GIBO requires less than half of the queries from the real robot to reach $80\%$ of the maximal reward.
elements.sent-2411-372: To further investigate the significance of sim-to-real transfer, we ran the HCI-GIBO algorithm with 200 evaluations on the simulator, transferred the optimal learned policy to the robot, and then measured the performance in the real world.
elements.sent-2411-373: We show the trade-off between queries from the simulator and the robot in \fig\ref{fig:trade_off .}
elements.sent-2411-374: In this figure, we observe that, as expected, S-HCI-GIBO appears to query \(f_{\simu}\) more frequently than \(f\) at the beginning of the learning process.
elements.sent-2411-375: This makes sense as it is designed to start with the simulator and exhaust the potential information from this cheaper source.
elements.sent-2411-376: Interestingly, after eight policy updates, we observe a steep decrease in the number of queries from the simulator.
elements.sent-2411-377: We attribute this to the much shallower gradients where additional data from the simulator is not able to reduce gradient uncertainty.
elements.figure-2411-015: \includegraphics[width=3.5in]{figs/trajectory.pdf} \caption{Improvement: The trajectory of the pole tip after each gradient step converges towards the desired one.} \label{fig:trajectory}

elements.sent-2411-379: The trajectory becomes closer to its reference in each policy update.
elements.sent-2411-380: In this work, we introduce a novel zero-order optimizer (HCI-GIBO) and an extension to incorporate additional data from simulations (S-HCI-GIBO).
elements.sent-2411-381: Both algorithms improve the sample efficiency for black-box robot learning tasks and overcome difficulties in solving high dimensional problems by BO, a popular controller tuning method.
elements.sent-2411-382: The HCI-GIBO method employs an active sampling strategy that reduces the uncertainties of gradient estimates, quantifies the improvement confidence to adaptively control the required queries for policy updates, and leverages the information from a simulator.
elements.sent-2411-383: Our large-scale synthetic experiment demonstrates that the proposed algorithms outperform the baseline algorithms, especially in higher dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-384: The robot experiment results show that these algorithms are applicable to real-world tasks.
elements.sent-2411-385: Our proposed methods rely on local optimization, and inherit their local convergence.
elements.sent-2411-386: As such, the learning outcome depends on the initial parameters and it may be difficult to learn complex behavior with uniformed initialization.
elements.sent-2411-387: The proposed methods work best when they are used to fine-tune policies, e.g., initialized by RL or a human expert.
elements.sent-2411-388: Another potential drawback of our approach are Lipschitz assumptions required for the theoretical results.
elements.sent-2411-389: However, there are some preliminary results that show GPs are also able to estimate sub-gradients \cite{wu2023behavior .}
elements.sent-2411-390: More generally, the data-efficiency of BO methods is also a function of the prior knowledge of the objective; only informed priors lead to the high data-efficiency required when learning on hardware.
elements.sent-2411-391: A direction for future research is to extend the convergence proof for GIBO \cite{wu2023behavior to the multi-fidelity setting.}
elements.sent-2411-392: Currently our proposed methods are limited to use only one simulator, and we are working on a scheme that supports more information sources.
elements.sent-2411-393: Given that safety in robot learning is crucial, we plan to investigate how to extend the proposed methods for constrained optimization problems.
elements.sent-2411-394: This could be achieved by using Lagrangian relaxation.
elements.sent-2411-395: Further, to evaluate the efficacy of S-HCI-GIBO, we are interested in applying the proposed methods to different robot learning problems.
